text: Hello, I'm sharing a right colectomy for a cancer that was found during ongoing workup for a lymphoma. The patient had a PET scan for her lymphoma workup and found a sequel lesion or an ascending colon lesion. And you can see here, huge matted retroperitoneal nodes all the way across the central abdomen, periaortic. And this is an elderly, infirm person. She was anemic from the colon cancer, was elected to do a resection.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to perform a lower ligation on the iliac colic pedicle, avoiding the more central superior mesenteric vessels. Large matted nodes are visible beneath the vessel sealer.

The surgeon observes a fibrotic and scarred retroperitoneal plane, with the mid-ascending colon adhered to the side wall, suggesting acute inflammation rather than malignancy. Pus is visible, indicating a perforated retrocecal appendix, with the tip of the appendix exposed. The perforation appears unrelated to cancer, suggesting appendicitis.

The surgeon attempts to detach the mid-ascending colon from the sidewall, performing lateral mobilization without ureteral catheters. The hepatic flexure is mobilized, and the surgeon joins previous lateral mobilization efforts to address the perforated adhesed segment. The right colon mesentery is dissected, avoiding high ligation, focusing on tumor removal rather than nodal involvement.
The surgeon completes the lateral mobilization of the colon, taking additional mesentery to ensure clean margins. The perforated appendix is visible hanging down from the surgical field.

The surgeon staples the super pubic region and the small bowel, aligning an isoparastaltic anastomosis. A colotomy and small bowel enterotomy are visible, followed by the closure of these openings with sutures.

You know, overall, I think that this case went smoothly. It highlights a couple of principles. tailor your operation to the patient. My discussions with the family prior to this operation, we already knew we were not going to do an oncologic operation over concerns of the central mesentery given the lymphadenopathy, as well as the patient's oncologic outcome from a standpoint of the lymphoma. which they needed to start treatment on. Interestingly, I got out close to thirty five lymph nodes during this surgery and every single one of them was involved with lymphoma, not colon cancer. The other thing is what to do with the unexpected finding of the perforation. You know, my biggest concern initially was that this was cancer and I would need to you know, try to carve out as much of that sidewall as I possibly could and mark it with clips, sending some frozen sections, confirmed that there was no malignancy. I did mark the area with clips anyway, but permanent section proved that it was just appendicitis and not a cancer perforation, which was great for the patient.
The surgeon finishes closing the common channel without leaving a drain, and upsizes the twelve millimeter trocar site for phanaceal extraction.
